biasQuiz.question: You're down 1.5% on a trade and it just hit your pre-planned stop loss. What's the disciplined move?
options: Move the stop lower to give it 'room to breathe'
Close the trade — the stop is your decision made in a calm state
Add to the position to lower your average
Remove the stop and watch it manually
biasQuiz.explanation: Your stop was set with a clear head before emotions were involved. Moving or removing it is loss aversion talking — the urge to avoid realising a small, planned loss is exactly what turns it into a large, unplanned one.

biasQuiz.question: A coin is up 40% on the day and your feed is full of it. You have no setup. What do you do?
options: Buy now before it runs further
Buy half size to 'not miss out'
Skip it — no setup, no trade
biasQuiz.explanation: There will always be another trade. Chasing a move you have no plan for is FOMO. The best traders are comfortable missing moves that don't fit their system.

biasQuiz.question: You just took two losses in a row. The urge to 'win it back' immediately is strongest sign of…
biasQuiz.explanation: Trading to recover a loss rather than because a valid setup appeared is revenge trading. It usually leads to oversized, low-quality trades. The fix: a hard rule to stop after N losses.

biasQuiz.question: You're long and only reading bullish takes while ignoring bearish data. This is…
biasQuiz.explanation: Seeking only information that supports your existing position is confirmation bias. Actively look for the strongest argument against your trade before and during it.

biasQuiz.question: Your last 3 breakout trades worked, so you size up massively on the 4th. The flaw?
options: Recency bias — a small streak isn't a new edge
biasQuiz.explanation: A handful of recent wins feels like proof, but variance is normal. Position size should follow your tested risk rules, not your most recent results.

biasQuiz.question: You won't sell a loser because you're 'waiting to get back to breakeven'. This is…
biasQuiz.explanation: The market doesn't know or care about your entry price. Anchoring to breakeven keeps you in bad trades. Judge the position on its current setup, not your cost basis.

biasQuiz.question: After a big winning week you decide to trade without stops 'because you're reading the market well'. The risk?
options: Overconfidence — one trade can erase the week
biasQuiz.explanation: Winning streaks breed overconfidence and rule-breaking. The trades that blow up accounts almost always happen right after a run of success, when discipline slips.

biasQuiz.question: You've spent hours analysing a setup that no longer triggers. You take it anyway because of the effort. That's…
biasQuiz.explanation: Time already spent is gone whether you trade or not. Taking an invalid setup to justify the effort is the sunk cost fallacy. Only the present setup quality matters.

chartCall.context: Uptrend pulling back to support with a long lower wick (rejection).
chartCall.explanation: A pullback into prior support with a long lower wick shows buyers stepping in. In an established uptrend this often resolves to the upside — but always wait for confirmation.

chartCall.context: Lower highs and lower lows — a clean downtrend with a weak bounce.
chartCall.explanation: Lower highs and lower lows define a downtrend. The small green bounce is a weak counter-move; the path of least resistance stays down until structure breaks.

chartCall.context: Tight range / consolidation, small candles, no clear direction.
chartCall.explanation: Overlapping small-bodied candles in a tight range mean indecision. The highest-probability call is no clear edge — wait for a breakout rather than guessing direction.

chartCall.context: Strong momentum breakout above resistance on an expanding green candle.
chartCall.explanation: A wide-range candle breaking above a clear ceiling on expanding momentum tends to see continuation as breakout traders pile in and shorts cover.

chartCall.context: Climactic spike then a large bearish engulfing candle at the highs.
chartCall.explanation: A parabolic run followed by a large bearish engulfing candle signals exhaustion — buyers are trapped at the highs. Reversals from climax tops are common.

chartCall.context: Higher lows pressing into flat resistance (ascending triangle).
chartCall.explanation: Higher lows compressing against flat resistance is an ascending triangle — buyers are increasingly aggressive. These more often break upward, though a failed break is always possible.

disciplineScenarios.scenario: Price gaps hard against your position at the open, blowing through your stop before it could fill. You're now down 2x your planned risk. What do you do?

options.text: Exit immediately at market and accept the loss
options.feedback: Correct. The trade is invalidated and you can't un-gap. Take the loss, protect capital, and reset. Bigger-than-planned losses happen — adding to them is the real danger.

options.text: Average down to recover faster
options.feedback: Dangerous. Adding to a losing position that's already broken your risk plan is how small losses become account-ending ones.

options.text: Remove the stop and 'give it time'
options.feedback: This converts a defined loss into an undefined one. No stop = no risk control.

options.text: Hold and hope it comes back
options.feedback: Hope isn't a plan. Sometimes it recovers, but you've handed control to luck rather than process.

disciplineScenarios.scenario: You've hit your daily loss limit of 3 trades down. A perfect-looking setup appears. What's the disciplined call?

options.text: Take it — it's too good to skip
options.feedback: Even a great setup isn't worth breaking your daily limit. Limits exist precisely for moments your judgment feels sharpest after losses.

options.text: Stop for the day — the limit is non-negotiable
options.feedback: Correct. Daily loss limits protect you from tilt and revenge trading. The setup will repeat; a blown limit compounds bad days.

options.text: Take it at double size to make the day back
options.feedback: Classic revenge trading. Sizing up to recover losses is the fastest path to a catastrophic day.

options.text: Take it but skip the stop
options.feedback: Two rule violations at once. This is exactly the behaviour limits are designed to prevent.

disciplineScenarios.scenario: Your trade is up nicely and approaching your take-profit target. Price stalls. What's the most process-driven action?

options.text: Hold to your planned target / trail your stop
options.feedback: Correct. Sticking to your plan (or a pre-defined trail) keeps your average winner large. Reacting to every stall erodes your edge.

options.text: Panic-sell the whole thing now
options.feedback: Banking profit isn't wrong, but exiting on fear rather than plan caps your winners and hurts long-term expectancy.

options.text: Move target much further out on hope
options.feedback: Moving targets on hope is greed. If structure supports it, fine — but not as an emotional reaction to a stall.

options.text: Add size near the target
options.feedback: Adding risk right at your exit zone inverts your risk/reward at the worst possible moment.

disciplineScenarios.scenario: A news event you didn't anticipate is about to drop in 2 minutes while you hold an open position. What's prudent?

options.text: Reduce or close to control event risk
options.feedback: Correct. Unmodellable binary event risk isn't an edge — it's a gamble. Reducing exposure keeps you in control of outcomes you can't predict.

options.text: Add size to profit from the move
options.feedback: Betting on a coin-flip news spike is gambling, not trading. The spread and volatility are stacked against you.

options.text: Do nothing and watch
options.feedback: Acceptable only if the event is small relative to your stop. Otherwise you're exposed to a move you can't control.

options.text: Remove your stop so you don't get wicked out
options.feedback: Removing protection right before maximum volatility is the opposite of risk management.

disciplineScenarios.scenario: You realise mid-trade you entered without defining your stop or target. What now?

options.text: Define a stop and target immediately based on structure
options.feedback: Good recovery. Imposing a plan now is far better than running blind. Ideally, never enter without them — log this as a process miss.

options.text: Close the trade since the entry broke process
options.feedback: Strongest discipline. An unplanned entry is a process failure; flattening and re-approaching with a real plan is the cleanest reset.

options.text: Keep winging it
options.feedback: Trading without a stop or target is pure gambling and the leading cause of oversized losses.

options.text: Size up to make it worthwhile
options.feedback: Compounding a process error with more risk is exactly backwards.

disciplineScenarios.scenario: You're flat and bored. The market is choppy with no clear setup. What's the pro move?

options.text: Force a trade to stay active
options.feedback: Boredom trades are negative-expectancy. Choppy, setup-less markets are where overtrading quietly bleeds accounts.

options.text: Wait — protect capital until a real setup forms
options.feedback: Correct. 'No trade' is a position. Patience in unclear conditions is one of the highest-ROI skills a trader has.

options.text: Lower your standards just this once
options.feedback: Lowering standards out of boredom erodes your edge. Your rules should be hardest to break exactly when you're itching to act.

options.text: Trade a random coin for fun
options.feedback: Entertainment trading mixes gambling with your capital. Keep play money and risk capital separate.
